elements.sent-2411-001: Existing methods to summarize posterior inference for mixture models focus on identifying a point estimate of the implied random partition for clustering, with density estimation as a secondary goal~\citep{wade2018bayesian,dahl2022search .}
elements.sent-2411-002: We propose a novel approach for summarizing posterior inference in nonparametric Bayesian mixture models, prioritizing density estimation of the mixing measure (or mixture) as an inference target.
elements.sent-2411-003: One of the key features is the model-agnostic nature of the approach, which remains valid under arbitrarily complex dependence structures in the underlying sampling model.
elements.sent-2411-004: Using a decision-theoretic framework, our method identifies a point estimate by minimizing posterior expected loss.
elements.sent-2411-005: A loss function is defined as discrepancy between mixing measures as the loss function.
elements.sent-2411-006: Estimating the mixing measure implies inference on the mixture density.
elements.sent-2411-007: Exploiting the discrete nature of the mixing measure, we use a version of sliced Wasserstein distance.
elements.sent-2411-008: We introduce two specific variants for Gaussian mixtures.
elements.sent-2411-009: The first, mixed sliced Wasserstein, applies generalized geodesic projections on the product of the Euclidean space and the manifold of symmetric positive definite matrices.
elements.sent-2411-010: The second, sliced mixture Wasserstein, leverages the linearity of Gaussian mixture measures for efficient projection.
elements.sent-2411-011: We propose an approach to summarize the posterior distribution on the random mixing measure in Bayesian nonparametric (BNP) mixture models~\citep{ghosal2017fundamentals .}
elements.sent-2411-012: By focusing on the mixing measure, the method fills a gap in the existing literature which focuses on summarizing the posterior distribution of implied random partitions of experimental units~\citep{wade2018bayesian,dahl2022search .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The proposed approach requires only posterior Monte Carlo samples of random mixing measures as input and provides a well-defined summary of these measures.
elements.sent-2411-014: Importantly, the approach is model-agnostic, accommodating any prior distributions on the random mixing measures, including those with dependent structures such as dependent Dirichlet process~\citep{quintana2022dependent,maceachern:99 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Using the resulting point estimate of the mixing measure, if desired, one can derive an estimate of the density function and the partition.
elements.sent-2411-016: Most approaches to posterior summarization for BNP mixture models assume a context of density estimation, conditioning on a sample \( \{Y_1, \ldots, Y_n\} \) from a mixture.

elements.section-2411-002: Point estimation of random mixing measures
elements.sent-2411-046: In this section, we present a new approach for obtaining a point estimate of random mixing measures.
elements.sent-2411-047: As discussed, without loss of generality we consider the following Bayesian nonparametric (BNP) mixture model as an example: $ Y_1, \ldots, Y_n \iid F, \quad F = f * G, \quad G \sim p(G), $ where $f$ is a kernel and $p(G)$ denotes a prior on the random mixing measure $G$.
elements.sent-2411-048: Our objective is to report a point estimate $\Gh$.
elements.sent-2411-049: The point estimate can then be used for any downstream data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-050: We first define the problem in Section~\ref{subsec:problem_setup and then discuss current options for optimal transport distances that can be used as the loss function in Section~\ref{subsec:otdistances .}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Under a decision-theoretic framework, the point estimate of the mixing measure $\Ghs$ minimizes posterior expected loss: \begin{equation}\label{eq:posterior_expected_loss} \Ghs = arg\min_{\Gh} E[D(G, \Gh) \mid Y_1, \ldots, Y_n],\end{equation} where $\mathcal{D : \PP(\Theta) \times \PP(\Theta) \to \Re_+$ represents a distance on the space of measures supported on $\Theta$, which is the support set of the mixing measure $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Since the posterior is usually intractable, the expectation in ~\eqref{eq:posterior_expected_loss is approximated using Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Given $M$ posterior Monte Carlo samples, $G_1, \ldots, G_M \sim p(G \mid Y_1, \ldots, Y_n)$, the optimization problem can be approximated as \begin{equation}\label{eq:approximated_expected_loss} \Ghs = arg\min_{\Gh} \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^M D(G_m, \Gh).\end{equation}. \partitle{Algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-054: We use a simple greedy procedure to solve the optimization problem in ~\eqref{eq:approximated_expected_loss .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Specifically, we construct an $M \times M$ matrix, where the entry at row $i$ and column $j$ represents the distance between the $i$-th and $j$-th posterior samples, i.e., $\mathcal{D (G_i, G_j)$.}
elements.sent-2411-056: We then identify the index $i^\star$ that minimizes the average distance across columns, $\frac{1 {M \sum_{j=1 ^M \mathcal{D (G_i, G_j)$, and return $G_{i^\star}$ as the greedy solution.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: This is equivalent to selecting the best posterior sample from the posterior Monte Carlo sample.
elements.sent-2411-058: In practice, truncating the mixing measure $G$ can accelerate computation, improve convergence and simplify implementation \citep{ishwaran2001gibbs .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Specifically, we can use the following truncated version of $G$: $\bar{G = \sum_{k=1 ^K \alpha_k \delta_{\theta_k}$ with $0 < K < \infty$.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Ideally, we want to choose the largest feasible value of $K$ to minimize the approximation error introduced by truncation.

elements.sent-2411-235: We present a new approach for Bayesian analysis of Bayesian Nonparametric (BNP) mixture models, focusing on summarizing the posterior distribution on the mixing measures.
elements.sent-2411-236: Our method minimizes the posterior expected loss using a discrepancy between measures as the loss function, utilizing the computationally scalable sliced Wasserstein distance.
elements.sent-2411-237: For Gaussian mixture models, we introduce two variants: mixed sliced Wasserstein (Mix-SW) and sliced mixture Wasserstein (SMix-W).
elements.sent-2411-238: Mix-SW uses generalized geodesic projection on the product of the Euclidean manifold and the manifold of symmetric positive definite matrices, providing a meaningful metric for comparing Gaussian mixing measures.
elements.sent-2411-239: SMix-W leverages the linearity of Gaussian mixtures for efficient projection.
elements.sent-2411-240: Empirical analyses show that our summarization approach yields more accurate density estimates while maintaining a good partition summary.
elements.sent-2411-241: Limitations of the proposed approach include the restriction to truncated mixtures and the potential suboptimality of the reported point estimate of the mixing measures, as the solution is limited to the Monte Carlo set of posterior samples.
elements.sent-2411-242: The simple sliced Wasserstein distance might not be optimal for all mixture models; therefore, different variants should be designed to exploit specific geometry, as we do for Gaussian mixtures.
elements.sent-2411-243: Future work will focus on refining the search algorithm to achieve better point estimation and extending the approach to more generalized Bayesian nonparametric (BNP) mixtures.. \bibliographystyle{jasa3 .}

elements.sent-2411-425: Summarizing random partition with the SALSO package (R implementation), takes about $2$ minutes for $1000$ MCMC posterior samples of $200$ cluster indices using any of the three losses (Binder, VI, omARI).
elements.sent-2411-426: The reason that summarizing mixing measure is slower is that the mixing measure contains more information than the cluster indices.
elements.sent-2411-427: The computation of summarizing the mixing measure depends on the truncation level $K$, the dimension $d$, but does not depend on the number of data points $n$.
elements.sent-2411-428: In contrast, summarizing the partition depends only on $n$.
elements.sent-2411-429: Therefore, with a very large $n$, summarizing the mixing measure might be more efficient.
elements.sent-2411-430: Once the point estimate of the mixing measure is evaluated, it takes only $1$ minutes to obtain the point estimate of the random partition and the density.
elements.sent-2411-431: In contrast, it takes around $5$ minutes to evaluate the density estimate with 10 more MCMC iterations starting from an estimated partition.
elements.sent-2411-432: Figure~\ref{fig:faithful shows the data, along with the density estimate and estimated partition obtained under different loss functions using the two approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-433: In particular, summarizing the random mixing measures with SW, Mix-SW, and SMix-W results in only about a \(3.38\%\) increase in expected Binder loss compared to the best expected Binder loss, and only about a \(3.37\%\) increase in expected omARI loss compared to the best expected omARI loss.
elements.sent-2411-434: For VI loss, the proposed approach results in an increase of about \(8.86\%\) in expected VI loss compared to the best expected VI loss.
elements.sent-2411-435: Table~\ref{tab:density_estimation_oldf reports the expected losses using total variation and \(SW_2\) distance for the density estimation.}
elements.sent-2411-436: Summarizing the mixing measure naturally leads to better density estimates than summarizing partitions first.
elements.sent-2411-437: In particular, the best partition-focused method reports a \(13.5\%\) higher expected total variation loss and \(19.23\%\) higher expected \(SW\) loss compared to Mix-SW and SMix-W.
elements.sent-2411-438: Furthermore, we find that SMix-W and Mix-SW compare favorably to SW.
elements.sent-2411-439: Finally, in Table~\ref{tab:mm_estimation_oldf we report the expected losses under \(SW_2\) distance, Mix-\(SW_2\) distance, and SMix-\(W_2\) distance for estimating the mixing measure.}

elements.sent-2411-441: We present a new approach for {summarizing posterior inference under Bayesian Nonparametric (BNP) mixture models.}
elements.sent-2411-442: Our method minimizes the posterior expected loss using a discrepancy between measures, utilizing the computationally scalable SW distance.
elements.sent-2411-443: Mix-SW uses generalized geodesic projection on the product of the Euclidean manifold and the manifold of symmetric positive definite matrices, providing a principled meaningful metric for comparing Gaussian mixing measures.
elements.sent-2411-444: Limitations of the proposed approach include the potential suboptimality of the reported point estimate of the mixing measures, as the solution is limited to the available posterior Monte Carlo samples.
elements.sent-2411-445: In addition, the conventional sliced Wasserstein distance might not be optimal for all mixture models; therefore, different variants should be designed to exploit specific geometry, as we do for Gaussian mixtures.
elements.sent-2411-446: In particular, it has been observed that clustering of high-dimensional data might be challenging~\citep{chandra2023escaping .}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Summarizing the posterior of mixing measure} \begin{algorithmic} \label{alg:summarization} \STATE Input: Posterior samples of the mixing measure $G_1,\ldots,G_M$ ($M\geq 2$), and discrepancy $\mathcal{D}$ (assumed to be symmetric). \STATE Initialize $C \in \Re^{M \times M}$ \FOR{$i=1$ to $M$} \FOR{$j=i+1$ to $M$} \STATE $C_{ij}=C_{ji}=\mathcal{D}(G_i,G_j)$ \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE Find $i^\star = \argmin_{i \in \{1,\ldots,M\}} \sum_{j=1}^M C_{ij}$ \STATE Return: $G_{i^\star}$. \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-469: Moreover, we also give pseudo algorithms for vectorized SW, Mix-SW, and SMix-W in Algorithm~\ref{alg:SW , Algorithm~\ref{alg:MixSW , and Algorithm~\ref{alg:SMixW in turn.}}}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Computational algorithm of vectorized sliced Wasserstein} \begin{algorithmic} \label{alg:SW} \STATE Input: Probability measures $G_1=\sum_{i=1}^{K_1} \alpha_i \delta_{(\mu_{1i},\Sigma_{1i})}$ and $G_2=\sum_{j=1}^{K_2} \beta_j \delta_{(\mu_{2j},\Sigma_{2j})}$, $p\geq 1$, function $V(\theta)$, and the number of projections $L$. \STATE Vectorize $V\sharp G_1 =\sum_{i=1}^{K_1} \alpha_i \delta_{V(\mu_{1i},\Sigma_{1i})}$ and $V\sharp G_2 =\sum_{j=1}^{K_2} \beta_j \delta_{V(\mu_{2j},\Sigma_{2j})}$ \FOR{$l=1$ to $L$} \STATE Sample $v_l \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathbb{S}^{d(d+1)-1})$ \STATE Compute $P_{v_l}\sharp V \sharp G_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{K_1} \alpha_i \delta_{v_l^\top V(\mu_{1i},\Sigma_{1i})}$ and $v_l\sharp V\sharp G_2 =\sum_{j=1}^{K_2} \beta_j \delta_{v_l^\top V(\mu_{2j},\Sigma_{2j})}$ \STATE Compute $\text{W}^p_p(P_{v_l}\sharp V \sharp G_1,P_{v_l}\sharp V \sharp G_2)$ as in~\eqref{eq:1DW}. \ENDFOR \STATE Return: $\widehat{SW}_p(G_1,G_2;L) = \left(\frac{1}{L}\sum_{l=1}^L \text{W}_p^p(P_{v_l}\sharp V \sharp G_1,P_{v_l}\sharp V \sharp G_2)\right)^{\frac{1}{p}}$. \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Computational algorithm of mixed sliced Wasserstein (Mix-SW)} \begin{algorithmic} \label{alg:MixSW} \STATE Input: Probability measures $G_1=\sum_{i=1}^{K_1} \alpha_i \delta_{(\mu_{1i},\Sigma_{1i})}$ and $G_2=\sum_{j=1}^{K_2} \beta_j \delta_{(\mu_{2j},\Sigma_{2j})}$, $p\geq 1$, and the number of projections $L$. \FOR{$l=1$ to $L$} \STATE Sample $v_l \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathbb{S}^{d-1}), A_l \sim \mathcal{U}(S_d(\Re)), w_l \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathbb{S})$. \STATE Set $V_{w,l} = (w_l,v_l,A_l)$. \STATE Compute $P_{V_{w,l}} \sharp G_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{K_1} \alpha_i \delta_{P_{V_{w,l}}(\mu_{1i},\Sigma_{1i})}$ and $P_{V_{w,l}} \sharp G_2 = \sum_{j=1}^{K_2} \beta_j \delta_{P_{V_{w,l}}(\mu_{2j},\Sigma_{2j})}$ as in~\eqref{prop:generalized_geodesic_projection}. \STATE Compute $\text{W}^p_p(P_{V_{w,l}} \sharp G_1,P_{V_{w,l}} \sharp G_2)$ as in~\eqref{eq:1DW}. \ENDFOR \STATE Return: $\widehat{\text{Mix-}SW}_p (G_1, G_2) = \left(\frac{1}{L} \sum_{l=1}^L W_p^p(P_{V_{w,l}} \sharp G_1, P_{V_{w,l}} \sharp G_2)\right)^{\frac{1}{p}}$. \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Computational algorithm of sliced mixture Wasserstein (SMix-W)} \begin{algorithmic} \label{alg:SMixW} \STATE Input: Probability measures $G_1=\sum_{i=1}^{K_1} \alpha_i \delta_{(\mu_{1i},\Sigma_{1i})}$ and $G_2=\sum_{j=1}^{K_2} \beta_j \delta_{(\mu_{2j},\Sigma_{2j})}$, $p\geq 1$, and the number of projections $L$. \FOR{$l=1$ to $L$} \STATE Sample $v_l \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathbb{S}^{d-1}), w_l \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathbb{S})$. \STATE Compute $P_{v_l,w_l} \sharp G_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{K_1} \alpha_i \delta_{P_{v_l,w_l}(\mu_{1i},\Sigma_{1i})}$ and $P_{v_l,w_l} \sharp G_2 = \sum_{j=1}^{K_2} \beta_j \delta_{P_{v_l,w_l}(\mu_{2j},\Sigma_{2j})}$ as in~\eqref{prop:generalized_geodesic_projection}. \STATE Compute $\text{W}^p_p(P_{v_l,w_l} \sharp G_1,P_{v_l,w_l}\sharp G_2)$ as in~\eqref{eq:1DW}. \ENDFOR \STATE Return: $\widehat{\text{SMix-}W}_p (G_1, G_2) = \left(\frac{1}{L} \sum_{l=1}^L W_p^p(P_{v_l,w_l} \sharp G_1, P_{v_l,w_l} \sharp G_2)\right)^{\frac{1}{p}}$. \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-471: Since $P_{V_w ((\mu,\Sig)) = w_1 \langle \mu,v\rangle+ w_2Trace(A\log \Sig)$ is a Borel meansurable, using Lemma 6 in ~\citep{paty2019subspace , we have: \begin{equation}W_p^p(P_{V_w} \sharp G_1, P_{V_w}\sharp G_2)] = \inf_{\pi_{V_w} \in \Pi(P_{V_w} \sharp G_1, P_{V_w}\sharp G_2)} \int_{\Re\times \Re} |x-y|^p d\pi_{V_w}(x,y) = \inf_{\pi \in \Pi( G_1, G_2)} \int_{\Re^d \times S_d^{++}(\Re) \times \Re^d \times S_d^{++}(\Re)} |P_{V_w}(\mu_1,\Sig_1)-P_{V_w}(\mu_2,\Sig_2)|^p d\pi((\mu_1,\Sig_1),(\mu_2,\Sig_2)) \leq \inf_{\pi \in \Pi( G_1, G_2)} \int_{\Re^d \times S_d^{++}(\Re) \times \Re^d \times S_d^{++}(\Re)} (|P_{V_w}(\mu_1,\Sig_1)-P_{V_w}(\mu_0,\Sig_0)| \quad + |P_{V_w}(\mu_0,\Sig_0)-P_{V_w}(\mu_2,\Sig_2)|)^p d\pi((\mu_1,\Sig_1),(\mu_2,\Sig_2)),\end{equation} where the inequality is due to the triangle inequality of the $\mathbb{L _1$ norm.}}}
elements.formula-2411-055: \begin{equation}W_p^p(P_{V_w} \sharp G_1, P_{V_w}\sharp G_2)] = \inf_{\pi_{V_w} \in \Pi(P_{V_w} \sharp G_1, P_{V_w}\sharp G_2)} \int_{\Re\times \Re} |x-y|^p d\pi_{V_w}(x,y) = \inf_{\pi \in \Pi( G_1, G_2)} \int_{\Re^d \times S_d^{++}(\Re) \times \Re^d \times S_d^{++}(\Re)} |P_{V_w}(\mu_1,\Sig_1)-P_{V_w}(\mu_2,\Sig_2)|^p d\pi((\mu_1,\Sig_1),(\mu_2,\Sig_2)) \leq \inf_{\pi \in \Pi( G_1, G_2)} \int_{\Re^d \times S_d^{++}(\Re) \times \Re^d \times S_d^{++}(\Re)} (|P_{V_w}(\mu_1,\Sig_1)-P_{V_w}(\mu_0,\Sig_0)| \quad + |P_{V_w}(\mu_0,\Sig_0)-P_{V_w}(\mu_2,\Sig_2)|)^p d\pi((\mu_1,\Sig_1),(\mu_2,\Sig_2)),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-472: Moreover, from the Cauchy–Schwarz's inequality, we have: \begin{equation}|P_{V_w}(\mu_1,\Sig_1)-P_{V_w}(\mu_0,\Sig_0)| =| w_1 \langle \mu_1,v\rangle+ w_2Trace(A\log \Sig_1) - w_1 \langle \mu_0,v\rangle- w_2Trace(A\log \Sig_0) | =|w_1 \langle \mu_1-\mu_0,v\rangle +w_2(Trace(A\log \Sig_1)-Trace(A\log \Sig_0)) | \leq \sqrt{w_1^2+w_2^2} \sqrt{\langle \mu_1-\mu_0,v\rangle^2 +(Trace(A (\log \Sig_1-\log \Sig_0))^2 } = \sqrt{\langle \mu_1-\mu_0,v\rangle^2 +(Trace(A (\log \Sig_1-\log \Sig_0))^2 } \leq \sqrt{\|v\|_2^2 \|\mu_1-\mu_0\|_2^2 +\|A\|_F^2 \|\log \Sig_1-\log \Sig_0\|_F^2} = \sqrt{ \|\mu_1-\mu_0\|_2^2 + \|\log \Sig_1-\log \Sig_0\|_F^2 } =c((\mu_1,\Sig_1),(\mu_0,\Sig_0)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-473: From the assumption, we get: \begin{equation}W_p^p(P_{V_w} \sharp G_1, P_{V_w}\sharp G_2)] \leq 2^{p-1} \left(\int_{\Re^d \times S_d^{++}(\Re)}c((\mu_1,\Sig_1),(\mu_0,\Sig_0))^p dG_1(\mu_1,\Sig_1) \right. \quad +\left. \int_{\Re^d \times S_d^{++}(\Re)}c((\mu_0,\Sig_0),(\mu_2,\Sig_2))^p dG_2(\mu_2,\Sig_2) \right) < \infty,\end{equation} which completes the proof.
elements.sent-2411-474: Moreover, from the Cauchy–Schwarz's inequality, we have: \begin{equation}|P_{v.w}(\mu_1,\Sig_1)-P_{v.w}(\mu_0,\Sig_0)| =| w_1 \langle v,\mu_1 \rangle+ w_2 \log(\sqrt{v^\top\Sig_1 v}) -w_1 \langle v,\mu_0 \rangle+ w_2 \log(\sqrt{v^\top\Sig_0 v})| =\left|w_1 \langle \mu_1-\mu_0,v\rangle +0.5w_2 \log\left(\frac{v^\top\Sig_1 v}{v^\top\Sig_0 v}\right) \right| \leq \sqrt{w_1^2+w_2^2} \sqrt{\langle \mu_1-\mu_0,v\rangle^2 +0.25 \log\left(\frac{v^\top\Sig_1 v}{v^\top\Sig_0 v}\right)^2 } = \sqrt{\langle \mu_1-\mu_0,v\rangle^2 +0.25 \log\left(\frac{v^\top\Sig_1 v}{v^\top\Sig_0 v}\right)^2 } \leq \sqrt{\|v\|_2^2 \|\mu_1-\mu_0\|_2^2 +0.25 \log\left(\max_{v}\frac{v^\top\Sig_1 v}{v^\top\Sig_0 v}\right)^2} = \sqrt{ \|\mu_1-\mu_0\|_2^2 + 0.25\log(\lambda_{max}(\Sig_1,\Sig_2))^2 } =c((\mu_1,\Sig_0),(\mu_0,\Sig_0)),\end{equation} where $\lambda_{max (\Sig_1,\Sig_0)$ is the largest eigenvalue of the generalized problem $\Sig_1 v = \lambda \Sig_0 v$.}
